title: Conductivity, Thermal Stability and Morphology of a New Z-IOPE Inorganic-Organic Network with the Formula [FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl]
abstract:
354
Conductivity, Thermal Stability and Morphology of a
New Z-IOPE Inorganic–Organic Network with the
Formula [FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl]
Vito Di Noto,* 1 Maurizio Fauri,2 Michele Vittadello,1 Sandra Lavina,1 Simone Biscazzo2
1 Dipartimento di Chimica Inorganica, Metallorganica ed Analitica, Università di Padova,
Via Loredan 4, I-35131 Padova, Italy
2 Dipartimento di Ingegneria Elettrica, Università di Padova, Via Gradenigo, 6/a, I-35131 Padova, Italy
Fax: +39 049 8275161; E-mail: vito.dinoto@unipd.it
Keywords: impedance spectroscopy; inorganic-organic materials; networks; polyelectrolytes; tin complexes;
Introduction
Up to now, studies of polymer electrolytes have revealed
their tremendous potential application in electrochemical
energy storage systems.[1] They appear to be particularly
adapted for use in sensors, fuel cells, photovoltaic cells,
electrochromic devices, smart electronics, laser media,
antistatic materials, and precursors for catalysis.[2] Indeed,
polymer electrolytes possess a range of characteristics
that contribute to their current substantial technological
value:[1–6] (a) low manufacturing cost and high perform-
ance; (b) volume and mass restriction; (c) structural, che-
mical, and electrochemical stability (potential window
between 2.5–3.5 V versus 2 V of aqueous electrolytes);
(d) low toxicity.
Classical
polymer
electrolytes
consist
of
organic
macromolecules[1, 2, 6, 7] (usually polyethereal) doped with
inorganic
salts.
In
order
to
obtain
materials
with
improved conductivity many authors focused their atten-
tion to the preparation of the so called (nano)composite
polymer electrolytes (CPE).[8–10] In general, these latter
systems consist of conventional polymer electrolytes
which contain inorganic species embedded into their
matrix. It was suggested that the inorganic species inter-
faced with the polymeric chains generate the “grain
boundaries” effect which is responsible of the significant
increase of the material conductivity (up to 10–5 S N cm–1)
at room temperature.[9, 10]
Full Paper: This paper reports accurate studies on the
morphology, thermal stability and electrical spectroscopy
of a zeolitic inorganic–organic polymer electrolyte (Z-
IOPE)
with
the
formula
[FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv(C2n-
H4n+2On+1)Kl]. This material was prepared by means of a
sol-gel process. A possible mechanism of the sol-gel pro-
cess is proposed. Scanning electron microscopy showed
that the Z-IOPE resembles a gummy paste with a rough
texture and grains on the surface of the bulk material.
Thermogravimetric investigations indicated that the mate-
rial is thermally stable up to approximately 2008C. A
detailed study of the mechanism of ion conduction in this
system was carried out using impedance spectroscopy in
the 20 Hz to 1 MHz range. The analysis of real and ima-
ginary components of conductivity spectra indicated that
a full characterization of the AC electrical response for
this Z-IOPE system requires an equivalent circuit analysis
for frequencies lower than 10 kHz and correlated ionic
motion analysis based on the Universal Power Law for
frequencies higher than 10 kHz. These studies demon-
strated that the Z-IOPE material conducts ionically by a
mechanism mainly regulated by segmental motion of the
host material, and that charge migration by ion hopping
between equivalent coordination sites is not to be comple-
tely excluded in the host network. Finally, the conductiv-
ity of 4.77610–5 S N cm–1 at 258C classifies this hybrid
inorganic–organic network as a good ionic conductor.
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2002, 203, No. 2
i WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, 69469 Weinheim 2002
1022-1352/2002/0201–0354$17.50+.50/0
SEM
micrograph
of
the
[FexSny(CH3)2y
(CN)zClv
(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl] Z-IOPE.
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2002, 203, 354–362
 Conductivity, Thermal Stability and Morphology of a New Z-IOPE Inorganic–Organic Network ...
355
Furthermore, to obtain microscopically homogeneous
hybrid inorganic–organic polymer electrolytes many
attempts have been made to introduce the inorganic
atoms into the skeleton of polyethereal macromole-
cules.[11–18] Thus, two innovative classes of inorganic–
organic hybrid ionic conductors where proposed. The first
class, organically modified ceramics as polymer electro-
lytes, groups all those materials synthesized by copoly-
merization of organic macromolecules with metal and
non-metal alkoxides.[11–14] The second type of hybrids are
the Z-IOPE materials (zeolitic inorganic–organic poly-
mer electrolytes) which are characterized by the follow-
ing structural properties:[15–18] a) the organic macromole-
cules are linked to one another by bridging inorganic
clusters; b) the inorganic clusters are formed by the
aggregation of two or more inorganic coordination com-
plexes; c) the inorganic clusters can be either negatively
or
positively
charged.
Moreover,
as
previously
reported,[15–18] the Z-IOPE networks are prepared via sol-
gel and gel-plastic transitions.
Recently in our laboratory a new Z-IOPE network based
on poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) 600, K4Fe(CN)6 and
(CH3)2SnCl2 has been prepared through a solegel transi-
tion.[18] By vibrational spectroscopic studies it was demon-
strated that this material of formula [FexSny(CN)zClv
(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl] is structurally constituted of clusters hav-
ing a net charge of –5 bonded together through PEG 600
bridges with a TGT (T = trans, G = gauche) conformation.
The charged clusters are formed by three tin octahedra
linked by one [Fe(CN)6]4– bridging anion.
The aim of this paper is to investigate the thermal sta-
bility, morphology, and electric properties of the Z-IOPE
prepared as described previously.[18] In order to under-
stand the mechanism of ionic migration in the bulk mate-
rial, the conductivity spectra of [FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv
(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl] are interpreted by a combined approach
based on the Universal Power Law (UPL) in the frame-
work of the jump-relaxation model, and by equivalent cir-
cuit analysis. The results thus obtained are correlated to
the structural determination previously reported.[18]
Experimental Part
Reagents
(CH3)2SnCl2 (Merck) and K4Fe(CN)6.3H2O (Alfa) were used
as received. PEG of molecular weight 600 (Aldrich, ACS
grade) was further dehydrated by standard methods and
sealed under argon on 4- activated molecular sieves to
avoid contamination by moisture. All transfer and handling
operations of final products were carried out in an argon
atmosphere.
Instruments and Methods
The analyses of Sn, Fe, and K were performed by inductively
coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) by
using the method of standard additions. The emission lines
were: k(Sn) = 189.926 nm, k(Fe) = 259.940 nm, k(K) =
766.490 nm. A Spectroflame Modula sequential and simulta-
neous ICP-AES spectrometer equipped with a capillary
cross-flow nebulizer was used (Spectro Analytical, Kleve,
Germany). Analytical determinations were performed using
a plasma power of 1.2 kW, a radiofrequency generator of
27.12 MHz and an argon gas flow with nebulizer, auxiliary,
and coolant set at 1, 0.5 and 14 L N min–1, respectively. Sam-
ple dissolution for the ICP-AES determinations was carried
out by microwave digestion of the material using a CEM
MDS-2100 microwave system. Mineralization was per-
formed by treating 115 mg of the product with a solution
consisting of 54 vol.-% HNO3 (70%), 31 vol.-% HCl (37%)
and 15 vol.-% H2O.
Thermogravimetric (TG) measurements were carried out
using a Perkin-Elmer TGS-2 thermobalance, operating in a
nitrogen atmosphere. The TG profile was recorded under a
working flux of 50 cm3 N min–1 and at a heating rate of
208C N min–1.
The morphology of the product was investigated using a
Cambridge Stereoscan 250 Mark 1 scanning electron micro-
scope (SEM) at an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. The sam-
ple was coated with graphite film using a Balzer mod. Med.
010(6) unit operating at 0.2 mbar from 20 to 808C.
Elemental X-ray fluorescent microanalyses were carried
out using a SEM EDAX PW9800 coupled with an energy-dis-
persive X-ray spectrometer equipped with a Si/Li detector.
AC impedance measurements were performed between
20 Hz and 1 MHz by means of a computer-controlled
HP4284A precision LCR meter. The sample in the form of a
pellet was placed in a homemade Teflon cell between two
cylindrical gold-plated stainless steel electrodes. The elec-
trode–electrolyte contact surface and the distance between
electrodes were measured using a micrometer. No corrections
for thermal expansion of the cell were carried out. The tem-
perature was measured with an accuracy greater than l0.18C.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were
carried out using a Perkin Elmer U5600ci spectrometer with
monochromatized AlKa radiation (1486.6 eV) at a working
pressure less than 1.8610–9 mbar. The spectrometer was
calibrated assuming that the binding energy (BE) of the Au
4f7/2 line was 83.9 eV with respect to the Fermi level. After a
Shirley-type background correction,[19] the obtained spectra
were fitted by a non-linear least square fitting program, with
Gaussian-Lorentzian profiles adopted for all the peaks. The
atomic compositions were evaluated by using sensitivity fac-
tors provided by U V5.4A software.
Initial measurements for each sample were obtained using
0–1300 eV range, followed by detailed scans for the C1s
and O1s regions. The Si2p photopeak of the silicon resin pre-
sent as a surface contaminant[20] was used as a charge com-
pensation peak with an assigned BE of 102.4 eV.
Synthesis of the Z-IOPE
The synthesis of the material investigated here is obtained
following the synthesis route previously proposed.[18] The
electrolytic complex was prepared by combining two sepa-
rate solutions (A and B).

V. Di Noto, M. Fauri, M. Vittadello, S. Lavina, S. Biscazzo
Solution A (transparent), was prepared by dissolving
0.3603 g (1.64610–3 mol) of (CH3)2SnCl2 in 2.27 g of
water, followed by the addition of 1.81 g of PEG 600. Solu-
tion B (transparent yellow) was obtained by dissolving
0.2970 g (7.0610–4 mol) K4Fe(CN)6 N 3H2O in 6.00 g of
doubly-distilled water followed by 2.01 g of PEG 600.
Mixing of solutions A and B resulted in the immediate for-
mation of a yellow gel, which slowly eliminated water. This
mixture was washed with water and filtered under an argon
atmosphere. Further traces of water were eliminated by dry-
ing the material at 508C and 10–5 mbar for one week. The
homogeneous yellow material thus obtained exhibited the
typical consistency of lubricant grease.
Analytical data and molar ratios nSn/nPEG, nK/nPEG and nCN/
nPEG for the polymer are reported in Table 1.
Mechanistic Hypotheses for the Z-IOPE Sol-Gel
As described in the Experimental Part, the Z-IOPE mate-
rial proposed here is obtained through a sol-gel transition
which takes place immediately after combining solutions
A and B. Solution A is composed of H2O, PEG 600 and
We believe that the sol-gel process takes place owing
to well-established reactions.[21, 22] Catenation is well
known in organo-tin chemistry; (CH3)2SnCl2 is strongly
associated via bridging anionic groups, which raise the
coordination number of Sn to 6 and promote its coordina-
tion geometry to an octahedron with trans-methyl
groups.[21, 22] In our case the anionic bridging group is iron
4–). On the basis of tin solution
chemistry and the structural model proposed for the Z-
IOPE[18] investigated here (Figure 1), we are convinced
that the sol-gel process takes place owing to three types
depicted in Scheme 1, occurs when (CH3)2SnCl2 is dis-
solved in solution A, with a number of equilibria giving
rise to the octahedral tin derivatives I and II. As shown in
Scheme 2, these octahedral complexes could further react
with other tin acquo-complexes in solution A, thus
producing the I9 and II9 species.
Mixing of solutions A and B triggers the reaction
shown in Scheme 3, which is responsible of the shrinkage
of the gel and expulsion of water from the bulk material.
This condensation reaction, called syneresis,[23] increases
the number of bridging bonds and causes the gel network

Conductivity, Thermal Stability and Morphology of a New Z-IOPE Inorganic–Organic Network ...
Thermal Stability, Morphology and XPS Investigations
The thermogram in Figure 2 indicates that the [FexSny
(CH3)2y(CN)zClv(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl] complex is thermally
stable up to ca. 2008C. At temperatures higher than
2008C, two mass eliminations were detected. The first
elimination was registered in the 200–4108C temperature
range and is attributed to the decomposition of the PEG
600 ligand present in the material.[16] The second mass
elimination, observed in the 730–8478C range, is asso-
ciated with the loss of both KCl by sublimation[24] and of
cyanogen (CN)2 due to the decomposition of cyanometal-
late complexes, as reported in the literature.[25, 26]
The morphology of the inorganic–organic hybrid was
investigated by SEM measurements (Figure 3a,b). Figure
3a shows that the material presents the appearence of a
gummy paste. However, the rough texture and presence
of grains in the bulk material is clearly evident upon
examination at a higher magnification (Figure 3b). The
grains exhibit a mean diameter of approximately 10 lm.
The homogeneity of the material was confirmed by
analysis of backscattering images and microanalysis car-
ried out on different parts of the material by X-ray fluor-
escence with dispersive spectroscopy (XRD-EDS). These
investigations revealed that the material does not incorpo-
rate microcrystals and that the elements C, O, Sn, Fe, N,
K, and Cl are uniformly distributed in the bulk material.
Therefore, we can conclude that the synthesized material
In an attempt to determine the oxidation states of the
metal atoms forming the inorganic–organic material, we
carried out XPS investigations. However, due to their low
concentrations, no metal atom peaks were detected. The
Reactions of derivatives I and II from Scheme 1 with tin octahedral aquo-complexes.
Condensation process occurring in the reaction
mixture after mixing solutions A and B (see Experimental Part).
V. Di Noto, M. Fauri, M. Vittadello, S. Lavina, S. Biscazzo
O1s peak associated with poly(ethylene glycol) presents a
single component peaking at 532.9 eV;[20] the full width
at half maximum (FWHM) of this band was 1.2 eV (Fig-
ure 4). A careful analysis of the C1s signal allowed us to
obtain interesting structural information. Indeed, two C1s
signal components were detected, the origin of which
were determined by fitting the bands by Lorentzian func-
The peak centered at 285.0 eV is characterized by a
FWHM of 1.1 eV, and, in agreement with literature
data,[16, 20] is attributed to the CN groups involved in the
formation of the mixed inorganic–organic network in
which both bridging and free cyanide groups are present.
It should be pointed out that the BE of this band was
detected at energies greater than that reported for the CN
groups in the matrix precursor K4Fe(CN)6.[20] The most
intense band, detected at 286.6 eV, exhibits a FWHM of
1.1 eV and was assigned to the C1s polyethereal carbon
Taken together, these results confirms that the pro-
posed material is a homogeneous inorganic–organic net-
work, with a good thermal stability.
The conductivity and charge transport mechanism in the
[FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl]
examined by impedance spectroscopy (IS) in the 20 Hz–
1 MHz frequency range and at temperatures ranging from
21 to 808C. The measured Nyquist plots are reported in
Figure 5a. A semicircular arc is observed at higher fre-
quencies and spikes are present at lower frequencies. The
temperature strongly influences the frequency range of
equivalent circuit models. Results indicated that the
impedance spectra are well fitted if an equivalent circuit
constituted by a parallel impedance in series with a con-
stant phase element impedance is used.[17, 28] The parallel
impedance is constituted by a resistance in parallel with a
capacitance. The capacitance of the parallel circuit, ca.
1.41610–10 F, was not affected by the sample tempera-
ture. At all temperatures the n values[27] of the CPE in ser-
ies with the parallel circuit ranged from 0.82 to 0.93, thus
indicating that the straight lines detected at low frequen-
cies could be associated with blocking electrode impe-
dancies, or space charge capacitance.[28] The bulk resis-
tances so determined were used to obtain the conductivity
as a function of temperature of the [FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)z
Clv(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl] Z-IOPE complex.
SEM micrographs of the [FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv
(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl] Z-IOPE: image b) is an enlargement of a).
XPS of the [FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv(C2nH4n+2On+1)Kl]
Conductivity, Thermal Stability and Morphology of a New Z-IOPE Inorganic–Organic Network ...
Figure 5b shows the plots of the imaginary impedance
component (–Z99) versus the logarithm of frequency.
These profiles exhibit typical Debye peaks[29] in the 4 f
log[f/Hz] f 6 frequency range. The frequency and the
intensity of the maximum of these Lorentzian shapes
depend on the temperature. To obtain the relationship
between the dielectric relaxation times speak = 1/fpeak and
temperature, the frequencies fpeak at the maximum of the
Debye peaks were determined by fitting these curves by
In accordance with previous work,[17] a full characteri-
zation of the AC response of the Z-IOPE material was
carried out by analyzing the real component of the con-
ductivity spectra (r9(x)). r9(x) was calculated using the
Equation: r9(x) = k N Z9(x)/[(Z9(x))2 + (Z99(x))2], where k
is the cell constant in cm–1. Figure 6a shows that the
r9(x) curves are characterized by three different regions:
a) the low frequency spike, b) the medium plateau, and c)
the high frequency spike. The low and medium frequency
portions of r9(x) spectra are well described by the param-
eters of the equivalent circuit described above. However,
in agreement with other studies,[17] the increase in r9(x)
at high frequencies was attributed to correlated ionic
motions in Z-IOPE bulk material and could be rationa-
lized by fitting the data with the UPL equation:[16, 17, 30]
r9(x) = r9(0) [1 + (x N s1) p]
where r9(0) denotes the DC current conductivity (r9(0) =
rDC), s1 is a time related to the initial site relaxation time
of ion hopping s2, p = s2/s* is the power law exponent,

It was reported[30] that conductivity due to ion hopping
is “successful” when the “initial site relaxation rate” = 1/
s2 = rs in the host material is higher than the “initial back-
hop rate” = 1/s* = rb of the ion. Interesting information
regarding this charge migration process was gained by

Taken together, the IS results reported here demon-
strate that the conductivity in the proposed Z-IOPE mate-
rials is ionic and mainly regulated by segmental motion
of the host material.[31] However, a limited contribution to
the overall charge migration mechanism by ion hop-
ping[30, 32, 33] between equivalent coordination sites is not
to be excluded. Finally, owing to its conductivity of ca.
described here can be considered a good ionic conductor.
This paper describes the physical properties of a Z-IOPE
system with the formula [FexSny(CH3)2y(CN)zClv(C2n-
H4n+2On+1)Kl] which was prepared by means of a sol-gel
process. On the basis of the analytical data, the previously
reported FT-IR[18] data and XPS studies it was concluded
that this polymer electrolyte is a mixed inorganic–
organic network in which charged clusters formed by 3
tin complexes and 1 cyanometallate anion complex are
bonded together by PEG 600 bridges. A possible mechan-
ism of reaction of the sol-gel transition is proposed on the
basis of the structural information available in literature
and of the well-known chemistry of tin derivatives. Ther-
mogravimetric measurements showed that the material is
thermally stable up to approximately 2008C. SEM analy-
sis of the Z-IOPE’s morphology revealed that it resem-
bles a gummy paste with a rough texture and grains in the
The impedance spectroscopy studies were carried out
in the 21–808C temperature range. The full characteriza-
tion of the conductivity spectra carried out on the base of
equivalent circuit analysis and of the Universal Power
Law approach in the framework of jump-relaxation
model, demonstrated that the Z-IOPE material conducts
ionically by a mechanism principally mediated by seg-
mental motion of the host material.
Finally, its conductivity of ca. 4.77610–5 S N cm–1 at
218C classifies this Z-IOPE as a good polymer electro-
